clean_story:
Stretched seconds. Warped memories.

Dream time distortion is the brain’s trick of making time feel slower or faster than it is—like a DVD on wrong-speed playback, but inside your head. It happens when your mind records moments unevenly: a near-miss car crash drags like syrup, while a boring lecture vanishes in a blink.

Here’s how it works: Your brain doesn’t track time like a clock. Instead, it stitches together snapshots of attention, emotion, and sensory details. When adrenaline spikes (like during fear or excitement), your brain logs more snapshots per second—a 5-second fall feels like 20. But when you’re bored, it skips frames, collapsing an hour into a haze. Tests show people overestimate scary events by 36% on average, while routine tasks shrink by 40%.

Extreme cases? Free-fall skydivers report "endless" 30-second drops. Trauma survivors describe minutes stretching into hours. Even dreams exploit this: a 10-second REM phase can unfold as a 20-minute epic, because your sleeping brain fills gaps with fabricated details.

Three related subjects are memory reconstruction tricks, adrenaline’s time-warping effects, and how animals perceive speed.
